_meta.note: Real Civil War papers per side, each with a political lean that sets how it reacts to the war's turns; reactions shift the paper's favor toward/against the administration; the aggregate sentiment feeds C.morale.public (the m3 home-front layer) anchored at 50=neutral. favorShift -> delta: strongly-for +8 / for +4 / neutral 0 / against -4 / strongly-against -8. Papers/editors/leanings are Verified standard records; voice/editorials enriched by the adversarially-verified s2-press-research workflow at commit.

papers.name: New-York Tribune
papers.voice: Thundering, moralizing, impatient radical-Republican broadsheet that treats emancipation as the war's whole point and lashes the administration whenever it dawdles.

editorials.headline: AT LAST! THE PRAYER OF TWENTY MILLIONS IS ANSWERED — THE BONDMAN GOES FREE
FORWARD TO RICHMOND! THE NATION WAITS WHILE GENERALS DAWDLE
STRIKE SLAVERY AT THE ROOT — IT IS PREPOSTEROUS TO SPARE THE THING THAT FEEDS THE REBELLION
THE CONSCRIPTION IS JUST — LET NO MOB OF SECESSION SYMPATHIZERS UNMAN THE REPUBLIC
DISASTER ON THE FIELD: THE COUNTRY DEMANDS COMMANDERS WHO WILL FIGHT, NOT RETREAT

papers.name: The Liberator
papers.voice: Uncompromising, scripture-tongued immediatist weekly that judges every act of the war solely by whether it brings the slave one step nearer to instant, unconditional freedom.

editorials.headline: GLORY TO GOD — THE PROCLAMATION IS SIGNED; LET THE JUBILEE OF THE BONDMAN BEGIN
HALF-MEASURES SAVE NOTHING: NO UNION WITH SLAVEHOLDERS, NO PEACE WHILE A CHAIN REMAINS
IMMEDIATE AND UNCONDITIONAL: ARM THE FREEDMAN AND LET HIM STRIKE FOR HIS OWN LIBERTY
THE SWORD OF FREEDOM PREVAILS — EVERY TRIUMPH IS A NAIL IN SLAVERY'S COFFIN
THE BLOOD OF THE FREE ATONES FOR THE NATION'S GUILT — WE DARE NOT FALTER NOW

papers.name: Chicago Tribune
papers.voice: Aggressive Western Republican organ — Medill held a one-third interest and ran it as managing editor — that helped build the new party and push Lincoln's 1860 nomination, demanding emancipation early, hard war always, and no compromise with the slave power.

editorials.headline: THE PROCLAMATION COMES TOO LATE BY A YEAR — BUT WELCOME, AND NOW PROSECUTE IT TO THE HILT
NO COMPROMISE WITH TRAITORS: CRUSH THE REBELLION AND THE SLAVERY THAT BORE IT
THE WEST REJOICES — OUR ARMIES PROVE THE UNION SHALL NOT BE DISSOLVED
FILL THE RANKS — ILLINOIS WILL NOT BE FOUND WANTING IN MEN FOR THE NATION'S LIFE
BLUNDERS IN HIGH COMMAND COST US DEAR — GIVE US GENERALS WHO MEAN TO WIN

papers.name: Harper's Weekly
papers.voice: Polished, illustrated 'Journal of Civilization' that began the war cautiously moderate on slavery — critics jeered 'Harper's Weakly' for courting Southern readers — then swung firmly behind Lincoln; once Curtis took the political editorship in 1863 it rallied the loyal North, ran the full Proclamation, glorified the Union in Nast's woodcuts, and pilloried Davis and the Copperheads.

editorials.headline: THE STARS AND STRIPES ADVANCE — THE UNION ARMS VINDICATE THE NATION'S FAITH
EMANCIPATION: A REMARKABLE CHANGE OF OPINION — THE NATION TAKES ITS STAND FOR FREEDOM
THE LAW MUST BE OBEYED — THE RIOTERS OF THE CITY DISGRACE THE LOYAL CAUSE
LET EUROPE BEWARE — NO FOREIGN POWER SHALL BLESS THE TRAITORS' CONFEDERACY
REVERSES THERE MAY BE, BUT THE PEOPLE STAND FIRM BEHIND THE PRESIDENT AND THE UNION

papers.name: Chicago Times
papers.voice: Vituperative, sneering, and openly white-supremacist — the loudest Copperhead sheet in the West, a paper Storey turned into a daily creed of anti-administration invective and naked racial demagoguery.

editorials.headline: A WAR FOR THE NEGRO. — The white man's blood spent to break his own chains and forge the black man's freedom.
CONSCRIPTION! — Freemen DRAGGED to the shambles to fight a quarrel of the Abolitionists. Resist this tyranny.
THE RIGHT OF FREE SPEECH HAS NOT PASSED AWAY. — We have, then, still a free press, in spite of the bayonet.
ANOTHER SLAUGHTER. — How many graves must be dug before the people end this hopeless and unholy carnage?
NO NEARER PEACE. — Two years of blood and the lines have not moved. The war cannot be won — let it END.

papers.name: New York World
papers.voice: Polished, lawyerly, and venomous — the organ of the New York Democracy, branding Lincoln a despot in elegant prose while peddling the rawest race-baiting of the war; its correspondents Croly and Wakeman coined the slur 'miscegenation' in 1864 to inflame white fear of emancipation.

editorials.headline: THE PROCLAMATION. — An unconstitutional usurpation that converts a war for the Union into a war for amalgamation.
THE CONSCRIPTION ACT. — A measure unknown to the Constitution; the people are not chattels of the War Office.
SEIZED BY THE BAYONET. — Our presses stopped, our editors threatened — is this the Republic, or the rule of Stanton?
THE COST OF THE WAR. — Defeat upon defeat, and still the Administration demands more men, more blood, more taxes.
THE OLD UNION OR NONE. — Let an armistice and the ballot, not the bayonet, restore the Union of our fathers.

papers.name: New-York Herald
papers.voice: Sensational, independent, and gleefully contrarian — Bennett owed no party, backing Frémont, Breckinridge, Lincoln and Grant by turns. Fiercely pro-Union and pro-victory after Sumter, yet pro-slavery in sympathy and hostile to emancipation, flaying Lincoln daily; the one paper the President himself read each morning.

editorials.headline: GLORIOUS NEWS! — Push on the columns; the rebellion reels. The Union shall be preserved whatever the cost.
THE BLUNDERS OF THE ADMINISTRATION. — Good soldiers betrayed by bad statesmen; give us generals, not politicians.
EMANCIPATION. — A radical and dangerous experiment; the Union, not the negro, is the object of this war.
FILL UP THE RANKS. — The draft is harsh medicine, but the armies MUST be fed if the Union is to live.
STRIKE THE REBELLION HARD. — No half-measures; whip the rebel armies into the dust and dictate peace at the cannon's mouth.

papers.name: The Crisis
papers.voice: Defiant, agrarian, and unrepentantly Peace-Democrat — the Western Copperhead's gospel, its office sacked by Union cavalry in March 1863 and Medary himself indicted for conspiracy, yet preaching armistice and 'the Union as it was' to the last while casting emancipation as the ruin of the white laborer.

editorials.headline: THE WAR PERVERTED. — Begun to save the Union, it is now a crusade for the negro and the ruin of the white laborer.
VALLANDIGHAM BANISHED! — A citizen seized for words alone. Free speech is dead where the soldier rules the editor.
THE CONSCRIPTION. — Ohio freemen will not be marched to a war for abolition at the point of the Federal bayonet.
BLOOD AND TREASURE WASTED. — Another army wrecked. How long will the people endure this useless butchery?
PEACE — THE UNION AS IT WAS. — The sword has failed; let an honorable armistice end what no battle can.

papers.name: Richmond Examiner
papers.voice: A mordant, duel-fighting rapier of a paper that backs slavery and secession but flays Davis's every blunder with caustic, states'-rights venom — the loudest organ of dissent in the Confederate capital.

editorials.headline: EVERY MISFORTUNE TRACED TO ONE HAND: Mr. Davis Meddles, Our Generals Bleed
ARBITRARY IMPRESSMENTS TOUCH THE PEOPLE'S PRIDE: Congress, Order This Plunder by Law or Not at All
THE PRESIDENT WOULD BE DICTATOR: We Did Not Quit One Tyranny to Crown Another in Richmond
THE VANDAL BURNS THE VALLEY: Sheridan Makes War on Barns and Women — Answer Fire With Fire
HAD MR. DAVIS BEEN A PLAIN PLANTER He Had Never Fancied Himself Fit to Move Armies From a Desk

papers.name: Richmond Enquirer
papers.voice: Virginia's oldest sheet and the President's organ — restrained, literary, and quick to scold 'newspaper generals' who second-guess the government in time of trial.

editorials.headline: THE WISDOM OF THE EXECUTIVE VINDICATED: Trust the Government, and the Republic Shall Stand
CONSCRIPTION IS THE PRICE OF NATIONHOOD: Let No Man Skulk Behind a Substitute While the Cause Bleeds
EDITORS WHO CARP IMPERIL THE STATE: In War the President Must Have a Free Hand
A REVERSE, NOT A RUIN: Faith in Mr. Davis and the Army Will Carry Us Through the Dark Hour
EUROPE CANNOT FOREVER AVERT HER EYES: Cotton and Valor Must Yet Compel a Hearing Abroad

papers.name: Charleston Mercury
papers.editor: R. Barnwell Rhett Jr. (editor); Robert Barnwell Rhett (owner)
papers.voice: The trumpet of the 'Father of Secession' — a pro-slavery, states'-rights fire-eater that demanded disunion first and then turned its lash on Davis for fighting too cautiously.

editorials.headline: COTTON IS KING — AND KINGS ARE OBEYED: Let Davis's Timid Diplomats Stand Aside
A DEFENSIVE WAR IS A LOSING WAR: The President Sits On His Hands While the Yankee Gathers Strength
CONSCRIPTION TRAMPLES STATE SOVEREIGNTY: We Seceded From Coercion, Not To Embrace It in Richmond
SO PERISH ALL WHO INVADE THE SOUTH: A Triumph Won Despite the Government, Not Because of It
RICHMOND'S BLUNDERS BLEED CAROLINA: Davis and His Pet Generals Answer for This Disaster

papers.name: Richmond Daily Dispatch
papers.editor: Hugh R. Pleasants (editor); James A. Cowardin (founder/proprietor)
papers.voice: The South's biggest penny daily — a plain, news-first sheet that cheers the cause and steadies morale rather than feud with the President; heavy on news, light on combative editorials.

editorials.headline: GLORIOUS NEWS FROM THE FRONT! Our Boys Sweep the Field — Richmond Rejoices
THE ENEMY MAKES WAR ON HOMES: Yankee Torch and Pillage Only Steel the People's Resolve
THE KILLED AND WOUNDED — A Mournful Column, Borne For Liberty's Sake
THE CONSCRIPT OFFICER'S NOTICE: Every Man To His Post — The Country Calls
REVERSES WILL COME, BUT COURAGE ENDURES: Steady, Citizens — The Cause Is Not Lost

teachingCards.title: The press and the people's will
teachingCards.claim: Newspapers were the great engine of public opinion North and South — and BOTH presses were bitterly divided, not the united fronts the Lost Cause imagines.

perspectives.text: A partisan press shaped morale and elections; Northern Copperhead sheets amplified defeatism while the radical press demanded emancipation and hard war.
The Confederate press, far from a propaganda monolith, savaged Davis's government — the Examiner and the Charleston Mercury were among his fiercest critics.
The image of a gallant, unified South is belied by its own newspapers, which raged at conscription, impressment, and the administration throughout the war.

sources: Andrews, The South Reports the Civil War (1970)
Harris, Blue & Gray in Black & White (1999)

teachingCards.title: The Radical Press Was Lincoln's Ally and His Sharpest Critic at Once
teachingCards.claim: The Union's radical and abolitionist newspapers did not simply cheer the administration; they pushed it relentlessly toward emancipation and hard war, praising Lincoln when he moved and lashing him when he hesitated. Their loyalty was conditional on the war becoming a war against slavery — and their target was the Confederacy built to perpetuate slavery and the Northern Copperheads who would have made peace with it.

perspectives.text: In 'The Prayer of Twenty Millions' (Aug. 1862) Greeley publicly demanded Lincoln enforce the Confiscation Acts and free the enslaved, calling it 'preposterous and futile' to crush the rebellion while sparing slavery — yet a year later he backed conscription so firmly that during the July 1863 draft riots an Irish working-class mob tried to burn the Tribune building and Greeley escaped through a side door.

perspectives.voice: William Lloyd Garrison, The Liberator (immediatist abolitionist)
perspectives.text: Garrison's Boston weekly judged the war by one measure — immediate, unconditional emancipation — and his slogan 'No Union with Slaveholders' meant he distrusted half-measures even from a Republican administration until the Proclamation made freedom the cause. A lifelong pacifist, he nonetheless subordinated that conviction to backing Lincoln once emancipation was on the line.

perspectives.voice: George William Curtis & Thomas Nast, Harper's Weekly (pro-administration)
perspectives.text: Harper's began the war cautiously moderate on slavery — mocked as 'Harper's Weakly' for protecting its Southern readership — then swung behind Lincoln, printed the full Emancipation Proclamation, and used Nast's woodcuts to glorify the Union and vilify the Confederacy and the Copperheads. It was the administration's most effective popular megaphone, more cheerleader than scold, and its anti-slavery posture was a wartime conversion, not an original creed.

teachingCards.title: The Copperhead Press: Dissent, Demagoguery, and the Limits of Wartime Liberty
teachingCards.claim: The Northern Peace-Democrat ('Copperhead') press opposed Lincoln's war by attacking emancipation, conscription, and the suspension of habeas corpus — but its opposition was bound up with open white supremacy, and the Lincoln administration met it with suppression that tested the Constitution it claimed to defend.

perspectives.text: We are not traitors but constitutionalists: the Union our fathers made is being destroyed by an Abolition war for the negro, by drafts unknown to the Constitution, and by a President who jails editors and exiles congressmen. Yet this same 'principled' dissent traded openly in racial fear — casting emancipation as the ruin of the white laborer and 'amalgamation' of the races. Storey's Chicago Times was openly white-supremacist; the World's own correspondents coined the slur 'miscegenation' in 1864 to terrify white voters. The racism is not incidental to the dissent — it is at its core, and must be named, not excused.

Save the Union first and last. Lincoln is no statesman and emancipation is a dangerous experiment — I owe no party and say so daily — but a Northerner who cheers Union defeat or counsels armistice serves the rebellion. Win the war hard, then argue the politics. (Bennett's own pro-slavery sympathies are part of this picture, not separate from it.)

A free press is sacred, but Burnside's suppression of the Chicago Times and the 1864 seizure of the World show how war pressures bend that principle. Lincoln himself revoked the Times order — and the World was seized for printing a forged presidential proclamation, not merely for its opinions — evidence that the line between disloyalty, fraud, and protected dissent was contested in real time.

Copperhead 'peace' was not neutral. To demand an armistice and 'the Union as it was' in 1863-64 was to demand a negotiated end that preserved slavery and risked Confederate independence. Understanding these papers means taking their constitutional arguments seriously while refusing to launder the white supremacy at their core — and refusing the Lost-Cause move of recasting pro-slavery dissent as mere principled libertarianism.

teachingCards.title: The Confederate Press Was at War With Itself
teachingCards.claim: There was no single 'gallant South' in print. Richmond's and Charleston's leading papers savaged each other and President Davis over conscription, impressment, defensive strategy, and habeas corpus — even as all of them defended slavery and secession and raged at Union 'hard war.' The fracture was real, and it was loudest among the most extreme secessionists, the fire-eaters who had demanded disunion first.

perspectives.text: Every military misfortune of the country is palpably and confessedly due to the personal interference of Mr. Davis — and these arbitrary impressments touch the people's pride and sense of justice.

perspectives.voice: Charleston Mercury (R. B. Rhett Jr., for owner Robert Barnwell Rhett)
perspectives.text: The fire-eater's organ that printed 'THE UNION IS DISSOLVED!' Cotton is King and the South must strike, not cower. A defensive war is a losing war; conscription out of Richmond is the coercion we seceded to escape.
Editors who carp imperil the state. In war the President must have a free hand — the 'newspaper generals' who order victories with their meals bear none of the responsibility he does.

perspectives.voice: Richmond Daily Dispatch (Hugh R. Pleasants, ed.; James A. Cowardin, proprietor)
perspectives.text: Glorious news from the front, and a mournful column of the fallen — the biggest penny daily kept morale up and feuds down, with a circulation equal to its three Richmond rivals combined.
